topic: You can sell AI implementation to businesses that are drowning in it
working_title: The $5.9 Billion Business You Can Start for $100
title_options: The $5.9 Billion Business You Can Start for $100
Businesses Are Paying Anyone Who Can Install AI (Here's the Whole Market)
AI Implementation: The Most Accessible AI Business Nobody Explains
thumbnail_concepts: Split frame: Accenture tower vs a laptop on a kitchen table. Big number center: '$5.9B → $2K'. Sage highlight on the arrow.
Plain black card, white type: 'They don't need AI built. They need it installed.' Small sage underline on 'installed'.
description_draft: Accenture booked $5.9 billion in generative AI work in a single year — but the exact same service exists at solo scale, and businesses at every size are buying. This episode breaks down the AI implementation business: who pays, what they pay for, the real numbers at the low end and the high end, the exact tool stack, and a step-by-step plan to a first client. Download the full blueprint (free): [LINK]. Sources in the pinned comment.

beats.vo_text: Last year, Accenture booked five point nine billion dollars of generative AI work. Not building AI — installing it. And the same service, at solo scale, is a two thousand dollar project a single operator can sell in a month.
beats.asset_hint: chart: Accenture GenAI bookings FY2024 vs FY2025 (~2x), then cut to a plain card: '$2,000 — solo version'
beats.source: CIO Dive / Constellation Research, Accenture FY2025

beats.vo_text: Here's the thesis. Every business now knows it's supposed to be using AI, and almost none of them know how. That gap between knowing and doing is a service business, and it's called implementation.
beats.asset_hint: slide: 'The Gap' — three bullets: knows it should / doesn't know how / will pay someone who does

beats.vo_text: You are not building models. You are not raising venture capital. You're taking tools that already exist and wiring them into how a real business runs — its intake, its follow-ups, its reporting. [POV: your one-line version of watching hospitality businesses buy technology they never fully installed]
beats.asset_hint: broll: small business office, point-of-sale, front desk

beats.vo_text: And the reason this is buildable by one person now is that the installation tools went no-code. What used to take a systems integrator and six months is a workflow you can stand up in an afternoon.
beats.asset_hint: screen_rec: n8n canvas, dragging a simple intake-to-CRM workflow together

beats.vo_text: Start at the top of the market, because it proves demand. Accenture's generative AI bookings roughly doubled to five point nine billion dollars in fiscal twenty twenty-five, and revenue from that work roughly tripled to two point seven billion.
beats.asset_hint: chart: two bars per year — GenAI bookings and revenue, FY2024 vs FY2025
beats.source: CIO Dive; Constellation Research; Accenture Annual Report 2025

beats.vo_text: Here's the detail that matters more: their overall new bookings were flat. The AI implementation work is the only thing growing. By late twenty twenty-five they stopped reporting it separately because it touched eighty percent of large deals.
beats.asset_hint: slide: 'Flat overall. AI doubled.' with the 80% stat beneath
beats.source: Constellation Research; CIO Dive, Q1 FY2026

beats.vo_text: Now the other end of the market. A reported model making the rounds: a solo automation agency at forty thousand dollars a month from five clients on retainers. I want to be careful here — that number comes from a creator-economy blog, not audited books. Treat it as the ceiling story, not the median.
beats.asset_hint: slide: reported solo agency math — 5 clients × $5K retainer, labeled 'REPORTED, unverified'
beats.source: Medium / The AI Studio, Mar 2026 — reported, unverified

beats.vo_text: The floor is better documented because it's boring: freelancers doing AI automation at five to six thousand a month on about twenty dollars of tooling. And pricing converges across every source I found — two to five thousand for a first project, five hundred to five thousand a month for retainers.
beats.asset_hint: chart: pricing ranges bar — first project / per-automation / monthly retainer
beats.source: Multiple creator reports; ranges consistent across sources — estimate

beats.vo_text: One more structural signal from the enterprise side: about half of these AI projects pull through additional data and systems work. Services attach to services. Your two thousand dollar install becomes a relationship, not a transaction. [POV: a sentence on how your hospitality product's installs always uncovered the next three problems]
beats.asset_hint: slide: 'The attach effect' — install → data cleanup → next workflow → retainer
beats.source: CIO Dive (data pull-through on ~1 in 2 GenAI projects)

beats.vo_text: So the same business exists at three scales: Accenture's billions, boutique agencies' reported six figures, and a freelancer's few thousand a month. The service is identical. The only variable is who's buying.
beats.asset_hint: chart: log-scale ladder — freelancer / boutique / Accenture with sourced figures

beats.vo_text: The whole stack costs less than a hundred dollars a month. The brain is Claude or ChatGPT at twenty to forty dollars — that's your analysis, your drafts, and increasingly the build itself.
beats.asset_hint: slide: stack layer 1 — 'The brain', logos and prices

beats.vo_text: The runtime is n8n, Make, or Zapier — the thing that actually executes workflows for the client. n8n self-hosted is close to free, which matters when you're quoting margins.
beats.asset_hint: screen_rec: n8n workflow executing — trigger fires, steps light up

beats.vo_text: The client-facing layer is Airtable or Notion — the dashboard they log into and feel ownership of. This is the part clients think they're paying for, so make it clean.
beats.asset_hint: screen_rec: simple Notion client dashboard

beats.vo_text: And Loom for async delivery — recorded walkthroughs instead of meetings. Every tool here has a free tier, so your cost of goods on a two thousand dollar project rounds to zero.
beats.asset_hint: slide: full stack summary with monthly total under $100

beats.vo_text: The playbook. Week one: pick one vertical you've actually worked in. Not 'small businesses' — one industry whose problems you can name from memory. Every source on this business converges on vertical focus. [POV: name your vertical and why you'd pick it]
beats.asset_hint: slide: 'Pick the industry you can argue with'

beats.vo_text: Weeks two to four: build two or three real case studies. Do them cheap or free for businesses you know. You're not donating work — you're buying proof, and proof is the only marketing this business needs.
beats.asset_hint: slide: 3 case-study slots — problem / workflow / result

beats.vo_text: Then package. A fixed-scope offer: one named problem, one price between two and five thousand, one deadline. Businesses don't buy 'automation' — they buy 'your missed calls get answered and booked.'
beats.asset_hint: slide: offer card — problem, price, deadline
beats.source: Pricing ranges per research brief — estimate

beats.vo_text: The first client comes from your existing network, and that isn't a hack, it's the model. Operators buy from operators. Your credibility from the industry you left is the actual asset here.
beats.asset_hint: broll: handshake, small business owner at counter

beats.vo_text: And after the install, offer the retainer: maintenance plus one new workflow a month, five hundred to a few thousand depending on scope. Remember the attach effect — the first project is designed to surface the second.
beats.asset_hint: slide: project → retainer conversion diagram
beats.source: Retainer ranges per research brief — estimate

beats.vo_text: Now the honest math. Margins are real — your costs are tools under a hundred dollars and your time. That's how the ninety percent margin claims happen, and directionally they're right.
beats.asset_hint: chart: simple margin waterfall — revenue, tools, time

beats.vo_text: But a realistic first year is two to eight thousand a month, not forty. The forty thousand dollar stories are marketing artifacts from people selling courses about this exact business. My estimate, and I'm flagging it as one.
beats.asset_hint: slide: 'Realistic year one' range vs 'the YouTube number', clearly labeled
beats.source: Estimate — reasoning from pricing ranges and solo capacity

beats.vo_text: Three failure modes. This is a service business — revenue stops when you stop, and retainers soften that but don't cure it. At three to five clients, losing one is losing a quarter of your income. And every automation you install is something you now maintain when the tools update.
beats.asset_hint: slide: three failure modes, plainly listed

beats.vo_text: What you actually get from this business is diversification: income that isn't a paycheck, a skill stack that compounds, and a client list that becomes distribution for whatever you build next. That's the trade.
beats.asset_hint: slide: 'The real output' — income / skills / optionality

beats.vo_text: The full blueprint — the offer templates, the stack setup, the case-study framework, every source — is free at the link below. If breakdowns like this are useful, subscribe. Build it. Own it. Operate it.
beats.asset_hint: slide: blueprint cover + URL, channel wordmark with tagline

sources.claim: Accenture $5.9B GenAI bookings (~2x YoY), $2.7B GenAI revenue (~3x YoY), FY2025
sources.source: CIO Dive; Constellation Research; Accenture Annual Report 2025

sources.claim: Accenture overall new bookings flat while GenAI grew; AI in ~80% of large deals by Q1 FY2026
~1 in 2 GenAI projects pulls through data-modernization work
Solo agency at ~$40K/mo (5 clients × $5K retainers, ~85% margin)
sources.source: Medium / The AI Studio, Mar 2026 — REPORTED, unverified

sources.claim: Pricing: $2–5K first projects; $5–50K per automation; $500–5K/mo retainers

blueprint_summary.idea: AI implementation services: install existing AI tools into how one vertical's businesses actually run — fixed-scope projects that convert to maintenance retainers.
blueprint_summary.who_its_for: Experienced operators/professionals with credibility in a specific industry who want a first income stream outside employment.
blueprint_summary.first_customer_plan: Pick the vertical you've operated in → 2–3 cheap/free case studies from your network → fixed-scope offer ($2–5K, one named problem) → convert to $500–5K/mo retainer via the attach effect.
blueprint_summary.realistic_economics: Costs <$100/mo; realistic year-one $2–8K/mo (estimate); margins high but it's a service business — revenue stops when you stop, client concentration and tool-update maintenance are the risks.
